attrs.alt: Microsoft Entra Internet Access traffic flow and policy enforcement

content.text: What is Microsoft Entra Internet Access?

Microsoft Entra Internet Access is the Security Service Edge (SSE) component of Global Secure Access that tunnels and inspects internet-bound traffic from Windows, macOS, iOS, and Android devices — and from branch offices via CPE or SD-WAN. It applies identity-aware web content filtering, FQDN-based block/allow rules, Tenant Restrictions v2, and Conditional Access policies at the Microsoft network edge without requiring a traditional on-premises proxy.

Microsoft Entra Internet Access requires the Global Secure Access client installed on endpoints (or CPE configured for branch routing), plus an Entra ID P1 licence and the Microsoft Entra Internet Access add-on licence.
Tunnels device traffic to the Microsoft SSE edge
Terminates tunnels, inspects traffic, applies filtering
Groups web category + FQDN + tenant restriction policies
Assigns a security profile to users or groups
Blocks sign-in to non-corporate tenants at the SSE layer
All traffic logs forwarded to Log Analytics
Enabling the Internet Access Traffic Profile
Before traffic is tunnelled, the Internet Access forwarding profile must be enabled in the tenant. This is a one-time tenant-level switch.

# Enable the Global Secure Access traffic forwarding profile for Internet Access
# Requires Entra ID P1 + Microsoft Entra Internet Access licence
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'NetworkAccess.ReadWrite.All'

# Enable Internet Access forwarding profile
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method PATCH `
  -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/networkAccess/forwardingProfiles' `
  -Body (@{
    trafficForwardingType = 'internet'
    state = 'enabled'
  } | ConvertTo-Json)

# Verify profile is enabled
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET `
  -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/networkAccess/forwardingProfiles' |
  Select-Object -ExpandProperty value |
  Where-Object { $_.trafficForwardingType -eq 'internet' } |
  Select-Object id, state, trafficForwardingType

Security profiles are the policy containers for Internet Access. Each profile holds one or more filtering policies (web category, FQDN, tenant restriction). Profiles are then assigned to users via Conditional Access session controls.
In addition to web category rules, you can block or allow specific FQDNs and wildcards. Allow policies take effect based on priority — a lower numeric priority number evaluates first.
Assigning a Security Profile via Conditional Access

Security profiles are attached to users or groups through a Conditional Access policy that uses the Global Secure Access session control. This means different employee groups can receive different filtering profiles — for example, executives bypass certain categories that contractors cannot.

Tenant Restrictions v2 prevents users from signing in to external Microsoft 365 tenants (personal accounts, competitor tenants, unknown SaaS apps) even when using their own browser. The restriction is enforced at the SSE edge, making it bypass-resistant unlike header-based Tenant Restrictions v1.
KQL: Traffic Monitoring in Log Analytics
Internet Access traffic logs flow into the NetworkAccessTraffic table in Log Analytics. Use KQL to analyse top destinations, blocked requests, and per-user volume.

// Internet Access traffic summary — top destinations by volume
NetworkAccessTraffic
| where TimeGenerated > ago(1d)
| where TrafficType == 'internet'
| summarize Bytes = sum(SentBytes + ReceivedBytes), Sessions = count()
    by DestinationFQDN, PolicyAction
| order by Bytes desc
| take 50

// Blocked requests — investigate what is being blocked
NetworkAccessTraffic
| where TimeGenerated > ago(7d)
| where TrafficType == 'internet'
| where PolicyAction == 'block'
| summarize Count = count(), Users = make_set(UserPrincipalName, 10)
    by DestinationFQDN, WebCategory
| order by Count desc
Identify users with unusually high bandwidth consumption or high blocked-request rates — both are operational indicators worth investigating.

// Per-user internet traffic breakdown — identify heavy users or anomalous destinations
NetworkAccessTraffic
| where TimeGenerated > ago(7d)
| where TrafficType == 'internet'
| summarize
    TotalGB      = round(sum(SentBytes + ReceivedBytes) / 1e9, 2),
    Sessions     = count(),
    BlockedCount = countif(PolicyAction == 'block'),
    Destinations = dcount(DestinationFQDN)
    by UserPrincipalName
| order by TotalGB desc
| take 25
Internet Access vs Private Access vs Traditional Proxy
Deploying the Global Secure Access Client via Intune
The GSA client must be installed on all managed Windows devices to ensure internet traffic is tunnelled through the SSE edge. Use Intune to deploy and enforce the client across the device fleet.

# Deploy the Global Secure Access Client via Intune (Windows)
# Step 1: Download the GSA Client MSI from Entra portal -> Global Secure Access -> Connect -> Client Download

# Step 2: Create a Windows Line-of-Business app in Intune
# Upload the MSI and set these install parameters:
#   Install command : msiexec /i GlobalSecureAccessClient.msi /quiet
#   Uninstall command: msiexec /x GlobalSecureAccessClient.msi /quiet
#   Detection rule  : Registry key HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Global Secure Access Client, value 'Version'

# Step 3: Assign to 'All Devices' or a pilot group
# Step 4: After installation, verify the client tunnel is active:
Get-Service -Name 'GlobalSecureAccess' | Select-Object Name, Status, StartType

# Check effective policy on the endpoint
Get-ItemProperty 'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Global Secure Access Client' |
  Select-Object Version, TenantId, PolicyUpdateTime

macOS and mobile clients are deployed via the Microsoft Enterprise SSO extension (Intune App Configuration) or Microsoft Defender for Endpoint integration. Branch offices use CPE IPsec tunnel configuration documented in the Global Secure Access portal.
Not all traffic should be routed through the SSE edge. Configure bypass rules for internal tools, on-premises resources, and legacy applications that are incompatible with TLS inspection.
Detecting Clients Not Routing Through GSA

Users who uninstall the GSA client or bypass it via a VPN will not appear in the NetworkAccessTraffic log. Cross-reference your expected managed device user list with actual traffic to detect coverage gaps.
Microsoft Entra Internet Access add-on (sold separately)
IPsec IKEv2 tunnel from CPE/SD-WAN to Microsoft SSE PoPs
Entra portal -> Global Secure Access (preview as of 2024)
Global Administrator or Security Administrator to configure; Network Access Administrator for day-2 operations

Microsoft Entra Internet Access tunnels internet-bound traffic through Microsoft's SSE edge, applying identity-aware web content filtering, FQDN rules, and Tenant Restrictions v2 without an on-premises proxy.
Traffic forwarding is controlled by the Internet Access forwarding profile; filtering policies are grouped into security profiles assigned per-user via Conditional Access session controls.
Web content filtering blocks traffic by category (malware, phishing, CIPA) or by specific FQDN — allow rules with lower priority numbers override block rules.
Tenant Restrictions v2 is enforced at the SSE edge, making it resistant to client-side bypass unlike the header-injection approach of v1.
All traffic telemetry flows into the NetworkAccessTraffic table in Log Analytics, enabling KQL-based analysis of top destinations, blocked requests, and per-user volume.
The Global Secure Access client must be deployed to all managed endpoints (via Intune) and branch offices must be configured for CPE/SD-WAN routing to ensure comprehensive coverage.
